title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | UA gives ex-official $71,000 MAT-SU: Agreement bars university from making disparaging comments. By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 12, 2001) Wasilla -- In resigning as head of the Matanuska-Susitna college this month, Stephen Sylvester collected about $71,000 and a promise from the state university system. His separation agreement forbids University of Alaska officials, including President Mark Hamilton and UAA Chancellor Lee Gorsuch, from saying anything disparaging about him. In addition to promising not to say anything bad about Sylvester's performance, the university has agreed to strike any negative comments about performance from his personnel file if he requests it, according to the eight-page agreement. The agreement also forbids Sylvester from makin…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Flood covers Tanacross graveyard By Rosemary Hoban Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 27, 2001) When people in Tanacross moved their village in the late 1970s, they left behind friends and family in the low-lying graveyard a stone's throw from a bend in the Tanana River. Now villagers are back and trying to save the old cemetery, submerged under the worst flooding in many years. Recent heavy rains and glacier melt on the river have combined to raise the river above its banks and, as of Thursday, the river was expected to rise an additional 6-8 inches. The flooding has filled the graveyard with knee-high water that threatens to expose graves and destroy the spirit houses that mark them, some more than 50 years old. "I wish we could send the water away," said village health aide Dia…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 4 killed in plane crash near Bettles SIGHTSEEING: Fairbanks physician was flying 3 friends from Outside. By Rosemary Hoban Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 18, 2001) A Fairbanks physician was killed, along with her three passengers, when the single-engine plane she was piloting crashed into a remote mountainside outside Bettles on Sunday. Dr. Nancy Lewis, 48, was flying the plane on a sightseeing tour with three friends from New Mexico who were visiting for what her husband called "their great Alaskan adventure." Lewis retired from a 19-year family medicine practice in December to take more time to travel and volunteer, said her husband, Hugh Rose, speaking by phone from their home Tuesday. Lewis was doing some part-time work in local clinics, he said. She was interested in trav…

title: Rich rewards in Seward
summary: Most salmon charter operators in Seward make their living landing Resurrection Bay's legendary silvers. Not Dianne Dubuc. Dubuc has opted to take advantage of another angling opportunity -- king salmon. Seward attracts thousands of anglers every summer, but Dubuc has been making a name for herself by hooking fat kings during the lean months of winter. And Dubuc's business has done well enough that she plans to focus on kings year-round, she said. While kings will never unseat silvers as Seward's signature salmon, the new fishery offers yet one more temptation for anglers. "The kings do add to the diversity," said Barry Stratton, Alaska Department of Fish and Game biologist. "But Seward is still the silver capital of Alaska -- although Valdez would argue with that." Diversity is why Seward …

title: Flash flood wipes out road, washes 18-wheeler downstream
summary: An 18-wheeler got taken for a swim in a rain-swollen creek north of Fairbanks on Thursday evening when the surging water washed out the roadbed at Mile 5 of the Dalton Highway, state transportation authorities said. No one was hurt, according to witnesses. Crews were hoping to salvage the truck, which remained stuck in the water of Lost Creek. The washout occurred in a side channel of the creek, seven miles up the gravel highway from Livengood and about 50 miles northwest of Fairbanks. The road remained closed overnight Thursday, stranding up to 20 tractor-trailers, several passenger vehicles and apparently a tour bus on the north side of the break, said Joel Craft, a maintenance manager for the Department of Transportation in Fairbanks. One lane of the highway was reopened at 10 a.m. Frid…

title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Japanese firm buys Gorton's A Japanese seafood company, Nippon Suisan, has purchased a major buyer of Alaska pollock, Gorton's of Gloucester, Mass. Nippon Suisan also bought BlueWater Seafoods, the second-largest retail frozen seafood brand in Canada. Nippon Suisan will pay $175 million for Anglo-Dutch consumer giant Unilever's North American seafood businesses, according Nippon Suisan. In Alaska, Nippon Suisan owns UniSea, which operates a major fish-processing plant at Dutch Harbor. Gorton's is known for products like frozen fish sticks and portions made from whitefish like Alaska pollock. Judge stymies Air Liquide sale U.S. District Judge H. Russel Holland in Anchorage denied a motion last week to modify a preliminary injunction blocking the possible sale of Air Liquide America Corp.'s …

title: Inside Alaska business
summary: Home buying program gets money Wells Fargo announced it received $20,000 from the Federal Home Loan Bank in Seattle for the 2001 Home$tart Saving Program. The program helps low- and moderate-income homebuyers who have not owned a home in the last three years come up with funds for a down payment and closing costs. The sponsoring institution matches each $1 the homebuyer saves with $3, up to $5,000. Applicants must save for a minimum of six months and may take no longer than two years to buy a home. The savings period is to develop budgeting skills, said community relations manager Jeri Walters. "We want to see a savings pattern," she said. Prospective buyers must also take a home buyer's class. MEETINGS High-Tech Business Council meets The Alaska High-Tech Business Council will meet at noo…

title: Wellhead value sold short in gas discussion
summary: Regardless of how Prudhoe Bay natural gas gets to market, the construction phase of the project will not bring anything like the employment booms that Alaska has seen in the not too distant past. (I have in mind the booms created by the Prudhoe Bay lease sale, by construction of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline system, and by the crude oil price explosion after the Iranian revolution of 1979). That point was the subject of my May 6 column. One reason I have re-emphasized that point is that there seems to be a clear preference by Alaska's political leadership for the gas transportation alternative that maximizes the number of jobs created during construction. A second basis for choice, one that is considerably more important than short-run job creation, has been getting little attention -- nam…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Comments sought on fisheries analysis Daily News Staff TY: Staff (Published: July 21, 2001) Public comments are due by July 25 on a huge government analysis on how the federal commercial fisheries affect the environment. The court-ordered, 3,500-page draft Programmatic Supplemental Environmental Impact Statement lays out different scenarios for conducting fisheries off Alaska. It is the first such analysis in about 20 years and is required under the National Environmental Policy Act. The analysis, designed to guide fisheries management for many years to come, has been widely criticized by both commercial fishermen and environmental activists. Several groups have Web sites with sample comment letters that can be sent to federal officials, who are refining the analysis. The groups incl…

title: Jet fuel leak in Nikiski is clamped
summary: A clamp has temporarily stanched a stream of jet fuel misting out of a leaking Tesoro pipeline in Nikiski. But the line won't be repaired and delivering fuel to Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport until tonight at the earliest, Tesoro officials said. Tesoro was awaiting a clamp that can handle the pipeline's pressure of 1,400 pounds per square inch before making the patch permanent. An unknown amount of the diesellike jet fuel has been squirting from pinholes near a weld in the 25-year-old underground pipeline, killing grass and polluting puddles in a utility corridor near Mile 35 of the Kenai Spur Highway inside Captain Cook State Recreation Area. Tesoro learned of the leak in the 70-mile underground pipeline Tuesday. It had spilled enough fuel to kill grass and pollute puddles al…

title: 14 saved in separate boating incidents
summary: Juneau -- Good Samaritans came to the rescue in two unrelated boating accidents in Southeast Alaska over the weekend, plucking at least 14 people from the ocean, the Coast Guard said Monday. In one accident, two families were picked up by nearby boats near Street Island, about 10 miles west of Ketchikan, after their 32-foot vessel caught fire and sank. The nine -- four adults and five children -- spent about 14 minutes in the water before being rescued by the Show Girl and the Sheltered Seas, said Coast Guard Petty Officer Roger W. Wetherell. No one was injured. The rescue boats had been dispatched from the nearby Sportsman's Cove Lodge after a distress call was sent from the vessel about 8:46 a.m., the Coast Guard said. It is unclear what caused the fire, the Coast Guard said. The nine ar…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska digest (Published: August 26, 2001) ANCHORAGE Cyclist is hospitalized Saturday after collision with SUV A collision between a motorcycle and a truck at the intersection of DeBarr and Muldoon roads left the motorcycle driver in critical condition Saturday night, Anchorage police and hospital spokeswomen said. A female passenger on the motorcycle was not as badly injured, police said. Her condition was not available from Providence Alaska Medical Center. The driver, a 43-year-old man, apparently was not wearing a helmet, said Anchorage police Lt. Steve Smith. It was unclear whether his passenger was wearing a helmet, but several witnesses said she was. The motorcycle was southbound on Muldoon when the accident occurred. The other vehicle, a Dodge Ram Charger driven by Walter G. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Challenges hit home in the Bush HOUSING Two-day program aims to raise awareness of unique needs in rural Alaska villages. By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 24, 2001) Tony Vaska comes from an Alaska village that is "very poor and very rural," and he wants bankers and housing advocates to hear about it. Vaska, a Yup'ik Eskimo from Kalskag in the Bethel area, is executive director of the Association of Alaska Housing Authorities, one of many Alaska housing leaders who on Thursday briefed more than 45 bankers, congressional staffers and other people about the challenges to providing rural Alaska housing. From staggering distances to heaving permafrost to pitiful poverty, financing and building a house in the Bush aren't as easy as in rural parts of the Lower 48. "…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Outdoor Hotline (Published: August 19, 2001) FISHING Record set for king salmon on rod, line, no reel Eagle River angler David Wagoner has set a record for catching a king salmon on the proverbial cane pole. The National Freshwater Fishing Hall of Fame has certified a 40-inch king he dragged out of the Indian River as its catch and release record for "Division #3 -- Pole/Line/No Reel." Wagoner, the holder of several salmon-fishing records at the Hall of Fame, had for some time wanted to show it was possible to catch a king without a reel. One of his sons, Keith, was also recognized this year for a fly-fishing record for 36-pound tippet. He horsed a 28-inch Russian River red salmon to shore in June with that cablelike line. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: August 23, 2001) Rocket launch delayed The scheduled Aug. 31 launch of a 62-foot-tall Athena I rocket from the state-owned Kodiak Launch Complex on Kodiak Island has been delayed while Lockheed Martin staffers work out technical problems, according to an official with the Alaska Aerospace Development Corp. The Kodiak Star mission is now tentatively set for liftoff on Sept. 17, AADC executive director Pat Ladner said Wednesday. The rocket will carry four small military and educational satellites for NASA. The Athena will be the first rocket launched from Alaska to put something in full orbit. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | People Mover bus driver cited for red-light collision that injures three ACCIDENT: Officials say third serious bus crash in two weeks is driver's fault. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 23, 2001) For the third time in less than two weeks, a People Mover bus was involved in a vehicle crash, the city said Wednesday. This time, however, the bus driver was at fault, running a red light in downtown Anchorage Tuesday afternoon and smacking into a taxi, said Bob Kniefel, director of the Municipal Department of Transportation. The cab driver and two women passengers were "slightly injured" and taken to Alaska Regional Hospital for treatment, Kniefel said. No one on the bus, including the driver and as many as eight passengers, was injured, he said. A dispatcher at Ancho…

summary: | | | Community Profile (Published: August 23, 2001) Ruby Population: 188 (2000) Location: On the south bank of the Yukon River, in the Kilbuck-Kuskokwim Mountains, about 50 air miles east of Galena and 230 air miles west of Fairbanks. Description: A traditional Athabaskan community where five of every six residents are wholly or partly Alaska Native. Subsistence activities provide most food sources. The city, school (47 students), Dineega Corp. and clinic are the largest employers. History: Ruby's current residents are Koyukon Athabaskans of the Nowitna-Koyukuk band, a nomadic group that followed game with the changing seasons. title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Deal goes to Alaska Airlines pilots Alaska Airlines said Wednesday it has reached a tentative agreement with the Air Line Pilots Association to grant the pilots big pay raises. The agreement calls for the 1,453 pilots to get an 11.05 percent pay increase retroactive to June 1, followed by a 5 percent increase next year and 4 percent increases in 2003 and 2004, the Seattle-based airline said. The agreement also would extend the current contract by two years, to 2005. ALPA's master executive council has endorsed the deal, which now goes to pilots for ratification, a vote expected to be completed by Aug. 30, the airline said. The pay issue arose as Alaska Air added Boeing 737-900 aircraft to its fleet. A neutral arbitrator ruled in May that pilots get the 11.05 and 5 percent raises for this y…

title: Fishing Report:Silver opportunities
summary: The silvers were home Wednesday evening, surging up Ship Creek in flashing waves on the incoming tide. The anglers were waiting. At nearly any given moment, bent fishing rods could be seen upstream and down as people struggled against the weight and power of bright, thrashing salmon. Silver salmon have long claimed a special spot in the hearts of Alaska anglers. Cherished for their strength, for their tasty fillets, and for their willingness to take flies, lures and baits, silvers traditionally start to appear in Southcentral streams by the middle of July. This week, a few were being caught in Bird Creek, the Little Susitna River, and in George Parks Highway streams from Willow Creek to Sunshine. But there is possibly no better freshwater action to be found for these hard-fighting salmon w…

title: Activist pushes project panel
summary: An Anchorage activist wants voters to create a state body that could finance, build and run a huge natural gas development project. Scott Heyworth, who has touted a project to convert gas to liquid form for export, on Monday filed to put the initiative on the November 2002 ballot. Heyworth said he wants the government body -- the Alaska Natural Gas Development Authority -- to have the power to issue bonds, contract with private builders to construct a pipeline and negotiate with the oil companies that hold most of the North Slope's huge, undeveloped natural gas reserves. He spent seven hours Saturday canvassing downtown and the Anchorage port, getting almost 200 signatures to begin the referendum process. Now comes the hard part. The governor and Alaska's oil companies have passed on a liq…

title: Kenai Peninsula waterway serves up fish, bears and beauty
summary: Cooper Landing -- About 11:30 on a clear July evening, Joe Naughton cast a brown caddis fly toward a fat rainbow trout prowling the headwaters of the Russian River. Across the stream, a brown bear was apparently eyeing the same fish. By the time Naughton looked up, the grizzly was already in midfuss. It thrashed toward him. The tourist from Salem, Ore., stumbled backward, waved his arms and stammered a few words. The bear turned tail, bolting back into the dense green underbrush. The forest resumed its almost hypnotic silence, broken only by the sound of the gurgling river and occasional buzz of mosquitoes. It's hardly the first time a fish-crazed angler has run headfirst into a hungry brown bear along the Russian. The clearwater river produces an astounding number of salmon, making it a p…

title: Discord with Canada threat to gas line
summary: Prices should remain high enough in the near future to bring Alaska natural gas to market, but the project could be derailed unless Alaskans do a better job of negotiating a pipeline route with Canada, an energy consultant said Tuesday. Ed Small, Calgary representative of Cambridge Energy Research Associates, told the Alaska Legislature's Joint Committee on Natural Gas Pipelines that the chance of a pipeline being built to transport North Slope gas to the Lower 48 was somewhere between 60 percent and 70 percent. "There is a window of opportunity for Alaskan natural gas," Small said. "It is not a done deal by any stretch of the imagination." He reminded the committee headed by Sen. John Torgerson, R-Kasilof, that regardless of what route is chosen, two-thirds of the line will be in Canada. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Government urged to settle auction NEXTWAVE: Bidders for wireless licenses offer possible solution to debacle By Kalpana Srinivasan The Associated Press (Published: July 26, 2001) Washington -- Three Alaska Native corporations and other big bidders in a $17 billion wireless auction -- the results of which were overturned by an appeals court -- are urging the government to settle the matter so they can keep their wireless licenses. The companies, which won the licenses in an auction that finished in January, laid out steps Wednesday that federal officials can take so that the U.S. Treasury still gets a sizable share of the auction money and other parties are compensated. That includes cutting a deal with NextWave Communications, the company that originally won the licenses for $4.7 bi…

title: Toohey's appointment sparks cry of conflict in house
summary: Arctic Village -- Interior Secretary Gale Norton got a polite welcome but a pointed message Monday when she visited this isolated Gwich'in Athabaskan village 150 miles north of the Arctic Circle. During a 4 1/2-hour meeting at the community hall, elders and tribal leaders made clear they oppose oil drilling in the coastal plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge, something Norton and her boss, President Bush, favor. Gwich'in Natives fear oil development will hurt the Porcupine caribou herd, which their people have subsisted on for thousands of years. "We want to continue this way of life here. We need to protect what we have, which includes the caribou," said Evon Peter, the young tribal chief of Arctic Village. Norton visited the refuge in late March, when it was covered with snow, an…

title: ANWR: 'Thou shalt not steal the future'
summary: Washington -- About 20 religious activists, most of them clergy members, got themselves arrested at the Department of Energy on Thursday to protest the Bush administration's support for oil drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. "We are saying this is a religious issue," said Jaydee Hanson, a United Methodist Church official. "We believe God wouldn't destroy all the parts of creation that the oil industry is destroying." A crowd of 80 or so came to the morning demonstration, across the street from the Smithsonian. Some held signs. "Defend Creation," said one. "Thou shalt not steal the future," said another. "Oy vey, I'm shvitzing. Put an end to global warming," read a third, borrowing the Yiddish word for "perspire." "We are here to pray and to bear witness for the Earth, and esp…

title: Big Oil sees chance to get ANWR
summary: With a pro-development president in the White House and high energy prices looming over the nation, supporters see a rare opportunity to open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil exploration. ''This may be the best chance to open ANWR in a dozen years,'' said Roger Herrera, from an office in Washington, D.C., where he is lobbying members of Congress to allow drilling in the refuge. The politics may be aligning to open the refuge, but oil development opponents promise a stiff fight. Opening the refuge's 1.5 million-acre coastal plain requires congressional approval, and how the vote would fall is unclear. Still, with the country feeling the pinch of high gasoline, oil and natural gas prices, supporters of oil development see the best moment since the 1989 Exxon Valdez spill to get leg…

title: Budget item creates ANWR stir
summary: Washington -- The latest scrimmage line over the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge -- at least for those in the House opposed to oil drilling there -- is a $5 million item in the proposed budget for the Department of the Interior. The Bush administration wants Congress to spend the money for plans and studies needed to hold the first oil lease sale on the refuge's coastal plain by 2004 and to expand exploration and development of the National Petroleum Reserve-Alaska, to the west of the refuge, according to the Interior Department budget request. Though the budget item would not in itself open ANWR, drilling opponents are mobilizing. Rep. Edward Markey, D-Mass, is gathering signatures on a letter urging the chairman of a House subcommittee to strike the item, or at least the ANWR portion of …

title: Is the coastal plain a blank spot on oilmen's map or an American Serengeti?
summary: Kaktovik -- Imagine this: You're in a small airplane buzzing through the vast Arctic emptiness. Clouds hang over the snow-crusted tundra. Sea ice stretches out of sight to the north. Imagine it, because if you are like 99.99 percent of Americans, you will never see this place yourself. Imagine it, because if you don't, then others will do so for you. This is the coastal plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge, where the idea of a place can be as important as its reality. Is this a promising oil field or an inspiring wilderness? An icy wasteland or sacred caribou calving grounds? As debate about oil drilling here revives, the coastal plain's fate will depend on which image of it prevails in far-off circles of power and public opinion. That is why, before the airplane touches down and A…

title: Anthology gives voice to ANWR
summary: Hank Lentfer lives in a cabin in Gustavus with wood heat, hand-pumped water and no appliances because he doesn't like the noise electrical conveniences make. He spends his time writing, making music and pursuing simplicity. So even he is surprised at how he spent his winter doing pretty much the opposite. In late January, with the newly elected president calling for drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge, an idea came that thrust Lentfer's life into the fast lane. That idea was to call upon a community of writers, scientists, educators, wilderness advocates, members of the Gwich'in nation and others to put together an anthology in support of keeping the refuge wild. Lentfer's first move was to call Carolyn Servid, writer and co-founder of the Island Institute of Sitka, to ask what…

title: Norton remains firm on ANWR
summary: On the last day of her second Alaska trip this year, Interior Secretary Gale Norton said that she had listened and understood the concerns of the Gwich'in people of Arctic Village but would not waver from her support for drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge. Norton suggested Wednesday that Arctic Village should broaden its world view. By continuing to oppose ANWR drilling, she said, Arctic Village is affecting the future of other children, from Kaktovik, where people want drilling, to the Lower 48, where people need oil to heat their homes. Arctic Village has become the poster village for the anti-drilling forces. When Norton visited there on Monday, Gwich'in people made it clear they oppose oil development in ANWR because they fear it will harm the Porcupine caribou herd, cruci…

title: Congress isn't ready to open ANWR
summary: Washington -- No one is admitting defeat or dancing in victory. Few are willing to publicly pronounce it dead. But the proposal to open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge doesn't look good. The coastal plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge stretches away to the north and east from a bend of the Canning River. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Even Alaska Sen. Ted Stevens, among the most ardent supporters of allowing oil drilling in ANWR, concedes Congress doesn't have the votes, not yet. "We'll get the votes," he vowed to reporters recently. "In my lifetime and yours, we'll get the votes. We don't have them right now, though. That's clear." And the Bush administration, which has put ANWR at the forefront of its energy policy, is giving mixed messages this week. The head of the Env…

title: Younger kids register today
summary: Registration for school starts today. For the second year, elementary school students will register in mid-August in an effort to get the school year off to a smooth start. Parents need to register their children according to the alphabetical schedule below. Middle school and high school students complete registration preliminaries at the end of the previous year. Students selected courses last spring, and their schedules will be activated during registration. Secondary school students will get their schedules and locker assignments at registration. Also, high school students will be able to purchase parking permits ($25 per semester) during registration. A recent vandalism incident at Goldenview Middle School will not affect registration, principal Connie Bensler said, adding that cleanup…

title: Tanaka's teens earn karate honors
summary: In a cavernous warehouse on a recent afternoon, three local teens practiced karate, while Sensei Katsutaka Tanaka watched like a hawk from the sidelines. The routines looked perfect, but the master teacher saw more. If ever there were a nick in a kick, a wobble in a block, hesitance in a stance, Tanaka would catch it. "Your mind and body have to work together. You have to be very calm. While you're moving, you have to be under control," he told Iris Mucha, who bowed to her teacher with an exclamation of "Osu!" (yes, sir) and continued practicing. Mucha, Vassar Bradford and Trey Jones recently qualified for the 2001-2002 USA Junior National Team. They'll go to the Junior Pan-American Karate Championship Nov. 23-25 in Trinidad. Before that, it's practice, practice and more practice. With uns…

title: PRINCE WILLIAM SOUNDBiologists report sea otters escaped diesel spill unharmed
summary: PRINCE WILLIAM SOUND Biologists report sea otters escaped diesel spill unharmed Most of the fuel spilled into Prince William Sound when the fishing vessel Windy Bay sank Aug. 4 has been recovered, agencies supervising the clean-up reported Friday, and 60 sea otters originally thought to be threatened are safe. Clean-up teams continued working Friday in the areas of Unakwik Point, Unakwik Cove, Fairmount Island, Little Fairmount Island, and Olsen Island. Bob Mattson, on-scene coordinator for the state Department of Environmental Conservation, said "things are looking a lot cleaner now." Shoreline clean-up crews are working on impacted areas, flushing the beaches and using absorbent materials to soak up the fuel. Six response vessels are recovering oil that remains in the water between Olsen…

title: Computers no help on Indian trusts
summary: Washington -- Interior Department officials misled a judge and Congress about the viability of a $40 million computer system meant to help fix a century's worth of mismanagement of Indian trust accounts, a court-appointed investigator said Thursday. The report by Joseph Kieffer detailed problems he found in the computer system being developed by the department that may force it to be scrapped. Such a move would be a serious blow to efforts to overhaul the $3 billion trust that manages royalties from Indian lands. Kieffer was appointed by U.S. District Judge Royce Lamberth to help oversee the changes to the trust. He criticized Interior officials, saying they have misled Congress and Lamberth about efforts to change it. "The Interior defendants presented testimony and evidence to this court…

title: Bludgeoning attack hospitalizes teenagers
summary: Two teenagers were hospitalized and several others injured in Palmer early Tuesday morning after an argument turned ugly during a night of drinking, Alaska State Troopers said. According to troopers, a group of teenagers was drinking and hanging out late Monday night and into Tuesday morning on a gravel bar in the Knik River frequently used as a party spot. Witnesses told troopers that a group of boys crashed the party and got into a fight, pushing and insulting one of the girls. After being chased away, the boys apparently returned with baseball bats and several other teens. Troopers said the suspects surprised the partyers and beat about a half-dozen people. Two boys were beaten unconscious and taken to Valley Hospital Medical Center by friends, where hospital staff notified troopers. Th…

title: Boat hit charted ledge
summary: The U.S. Coast Guard said Wednesday the boat that ran aground and sank in Prince William Sound Saturday hit a rock identified on charts of the area. The unnamed ledge sits less than a mile off the east coast of Olsen Island near the entrance to Unakwik Inlet. Until Wednesday the Coast Guard had not concluded whether the ledge was identified on maps. "This was a charted rock that is awash during some periods of low water," said Lt. Virginia Kammer with the Coast Guard in Valdez. Saturday morning, the 180-foot Windy Bay was headed south out of Unakwik Inlet when it hit the ledge. The tide was near its low ebb. As the tide ran back in, the Windy Bay refloated, destabilized, slipped off the ledge and sank about 500 yards southeast of the ledge in 1,000 feet of water, Kammer said. As the boat s…

title: 3-year-old girl dies of apparent accidental shooting, troopers say
summary: KIPNUK 3-year-old girl dies of apparent accidental shooting, troopers say A 3-year-old girl died Monday in Kipnuk as the result of an apparent accidental shooting, Alaska State Troopers said. Troopers from Bethel were sent to the village at 2 p.m. after receiving a report of the death. Kayla Carl died of a gunshot wound at her family's home. Troopers said preliminary information indicates a 14-year-old boy was holding a .410-gauge shotgun when it went off and struck the girl in the head. Troopers didn't know the relationship between the two children. Kipnuk, with a population of about 644, is 85 miles southwest of Bethel, four miles inland from the Bering Sea. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Heat wave hits Kodiak Island PARCHED: Dry, hot weather threatens wells, salmon runs. The Associated Press (Published: August 15, 2001) Kodiak -- Residents are hitting the beach in Kodiak, which is experiencing a spell of unusually warm, dry weather. "I can't remember a summer like this," said Gary Ennen, a meteorologist with the National Weather Service who has lived on the island for 50 years. The current heat wave, which should continue through the rest of the week, hasn't yet broken any daily records, however, Ennen said. Monday's high was 72 degrees -- five degrees lower than last year's record-breaking 77. title: Group starts gas line petition drive
summary: Juneau -- It doesn't have the money or the gas, but a grass-roots group has begun a drive to build a natural gas pipeline from the North Slope to Valdez. Citizens for the All-Alaskan Gasline Initiative is attempting to get an initiative on the 2002 general election ballot creating a governmental body that would oversee the 800-mile pipeline to Valdez, estimated to cost $7.3 billion. "I believe it is economically viable," said Scott Heyworth, who chairs the group. If approved by voters, the newly created Alaska Natural Gas Development Authority would search for markets to sell liquid natural gas in Asia and the United States at the same time that it searches for funding to construct the pipeline, Heyworth said. summary: Anchorage police are searching for a young man who raped a woman at gunpoint Sunday morning in Russian Jack Springs Park. The woman, whom police identified as in her 40s and from out of state, was walking alone about 8 a.m. in the park's south side. The wooded area, behind the golf course, has paved and dirt trails. Police declined to say exactly where the assault took place. The victim told police a man riding a bicycle came from behind her, held a short handgun to her head, forced her into the woods and raped her. She did not know the attacker, Detective Bill Morris said. title: Fish farming spurs concerns
summary: The amount of farmed fish produced worldwide doubled between 1989 and 1998, according to a report released Tuesday. The Pew Oceans Commission found that one of out every three fish that wind up as someone's dinner is now farmed. And in the United States, aquaculture provides almost all the catfish and trout eaten and nearly half of all shrimp and salmon. While fish farming helps to meet the nation's demand for fish, it's also creating a host of problems, including interbreeding, the spread of disease, pressure on small species that provide fish feed, and the discharge of waste into coastal waters, the report said. summary: The U.S. Coast Guard officer killed last week on St. Paul Island was buried Tuesday in his hometown. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska digest (Published: August 5, 2001) ANCHORAGE Copter picks up girl injured while hiking Crow Creek Pass A 14-year-old hiker and her party were stranded overnight in Crow Creek Pass after the girl injured her leg, Alaska State Troopers said. Friday evening, a party of five hikers on the Crow Creek trail were crossing a snow field when the girl fell, injuring her right leg. According to troopers, the girl, from Bar Harbor, Maine, believed she had broken her leg. Troopers said they did not know where the other hikers are from. A short time later, a sixth hiker came upon the group. He had a cellphone and used it to call for help. The Rescue Coordination Center and Life Flight were contacted, but neither service was able to fly a helicopter into the area Friday night because of bad …

title: One more measure of a life
summary: Susan Osborne got her picture taken on the worst day of her life, and they ran it on the front page of the newspaper. In the photograph, blood from police bullets stains her half-clothed body as helmeted officers haul her from a house where she had holed up for five hours after shooting an old friend in the hand. In the intervening two months, the woman she shot has forgiven her and asked that she not be prosecuted. But Osborne knows the score. "I'm going to do time," she said. The way she said it, sitting in a visitors room at the Hiland Mountain women's prison, it was not a complaint. It was just a fact, a measure of her life, one more bad outcome from one more set of bad decisions. She knows she's lucky no one died on Waldron Street that day. "I am not like that," she said of the woman …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Boat strikes reef, sinks WINDY BAY: All 5 crew members safe. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 5, 2001) A 180-foot fish tender with an estimated 30,000 gallons of diesel on board struck a Prince William Sound reef Saturday morning and eventually sank in deep water, its owner and the U.S Coast Guard reported. All five crew members of the Windy Bay hopped safely aboard a nearby fishing vessel, the Katy Ann, before the Windy Bay sank in 1,000 feet of water, according to the tender's owners. Response crews were hired by the Windy Bay's owner and the Coast Guard to contain a sheen of diesel rising from the wreckage. "It's bubbling a little, but they've got a skimmer on it right now. They've got everything boomed off," said the tender's owner, Ralph Hansen of Sumner, Wa…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Alors, darlings, it was awful. A nightmare. A Stephen King novel. Two innocents on their first date, lost on an ill-fated hike in the mountainous wilds of Alaska; alone, cut off from civilization as treacherous weather closes in. Will they survive? Of course, Channel 2 news producer Gina Anderson and her beau were on Stuckagain Heights, not in the Brooks Range. And they had a cellphone to call the troopers and mountain rescue people. And a gun they fired so searchers could locate them in the fog. As one might expect from a newsroom crew, Gina's colleagues were touchingly compassionate. As soon as they heard about her misadventure, they laughed hysterically and papered the place with directional aids -- big arrows that said things like "Gina, the studio is this way." title: North Slope Alaskans oppose gas route
summary: North Slope Inupiat Eskimos called on Alaska's big oil companies Thursday to abandon the idea of building a natural gas pipeline from the Prudhoe Bay oil field through the Beaufort Sea to Canada. At a hearing in Barrow that was at turns folksy, funny and stormy, Native leaders, Gov. Tony Knowles and numerous gas development advisers questioned oil companies' tentative plans for a pipeline development that would take natural gas out of Alaska with minimal benefit to the state. One by one, the leading political bodies of Alaska's Arctic laid down their opposition. The North Slope Borough, the Alaska Eskimo Whaling Commission and Arctic Slope Regional Corp. all oppose offshore route through the Beaufort Sea. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Union election scheduled for BP gas plant workers The Associated Press (Published: August 1, 2001) Kenai -- The National Labor Relations Board has scheduled a union election on Friday for workers building the BP gas-to-liquids pilot plant in Nikiski. Employees are being asked whether to unionize. Austin Industrial of Houston is the prime contractor and is the company that will negotiate with the unions if workers vote to unionize. As soon as the polls close, the votes will be counted. The results will be announced immediately. Both the company and workers have the right to challenge ballots and appeal the election results. If the employees vote to unionize, they will each have the opportunity to select one of the 17 unions of the Western Alaska Building Trades Council to represent th…

title: Police arrest suspect in slaying
summary: ANCHORAGE Police arrest suspect in slaying Anchorage police Sunday night arrested a man sought in an Eagle River slaying, a police spokesman said. Gabriel Leon, 23, was taken into custody in connection with the murder of a man outside an Eagle River home over the weekend. Police have charged Leon with first-degree murder in the death of Leneal Waters, 31, killed early Saturday. Waters was shot several times, including once in the head. Several people in the house told police they heard Waters having a loud argument with another man before the shots were fired about 5:15 a.m. The home where the shooting occurred belongs to the parents of a woman who was divorced from Leon and was Waters' ex-girlfriend, police said. Leon allegedly fled in a white car with a tan top that later was found by po…

title: Three bridges sitting in lot soon will span waterways in sloughs
summary: NORTH POLE Three bridges sitting in lot soon will span waterways in sloughs Three bridges housed at a Park & Sell lot on Badger Road have sparked the curiosity of passers-by, generating several inquiries as to whether the bridges are for sale. The answer is a resounding no, says Elaine Gross, a biologist with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's Northern Alaska Ecological Service. Gross spearheaded the purchase of the bridges so they can be placed in three locations where clogged culverts have backed up sloughs and all but stopped fish passage. With funding from the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation, Gross was able to buy the bridges from a timber project on Montague Island near Cordova. The bridges were barged to Valdez and then taken on trucks to North Pole. Moving the 46,000-pound b…

title: Firm recalls shellfish poisoning test kits
summary: The Alaska Science and Technology Foundation is asking people to return thousands of screening tests for paralytic shellfish poisoning after three tests failed to detect toxin levels that were higher than allowable limits. No one became ill because of wrong test results, said Bob Chaney, technology administrator with the state's science foundation. The failed results came from a product called MIST Alert, a screening test created by the Nova Scotia company Jellett Biotek. The Alaska Science and Technology Foundation contributed more than $450,000 to the product's research and development, Chaney said. The faulty kits came from lot number 40000-020101. The company and the foundation are recalling 3,100 of these kits -- 2,000 of which are in Alaska. Anyone who has one of these kits should ca…

summary: SUV hits customer chasing shoplifter By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 2, 2001) A man who tried to halt a shoplifter in Midtown on Wednesday afternoon was run down by the man's getaway vehicle. Henry McStotts, 49, was shopping at the Office Depot on Tudor Road about 12:30 p.m. when he noticed a man running out of the store with a computer printer, police said. McStotts joined store security in trying to grab the man, but the shoplifter jumped into the passenger side of a waiting Ford Explorer. McStotts stood in front of the Explorer in an attempt to stop the vehicle, police said, but the Explorer ran into him. He was dragged about 175 feet in the store's parking lot. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Habitat for Humanity helping six families come home CONSTRUCTION: Anchorage volunteers build six houses in Mountain View. By Asta Corley Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 3, 2001) For the first time in Anchorage, six Habitat for Humanity homes are being built concurrently on a single site. Two of the homes were completed earlier this summer on property across from Mountain View Elementary School. The others are almost done. "It's just a whole lot of energy out there when you've got a hundred-plus volunteers at any one time," said Tom Buskirk, board president for Habitat for Humanity Anchorage. "You can barely hear sometimes with all the hammers nailing away." Most of the volunteers are from Anchorage. "We're just generating this community spirit thing, which is really cool," Bu…

summary: Alaska Native march splinters By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 2, 2001) The "We The People" March -- an Anchorage event that usually showcases Alaska Native unity -- has competition this year. A group of frustrated parade organizers plan to hold an alternative march, dubbed "Strong Hearts to the Front," a few days after the fourth annual march in support of Alaska Native subsistence hunting and fishing. The first march is scheduled for Aug. 21; the second Aug. 24. The original march's co-chairwoman and several parade organizers resigned from an organizing committee in mid-July, citing disagreements over the purpose of the "We the People" march. This year's march centers on the state's handling of the Katie John subsistence lawsuit. John is an Athabaskan elder whose …

title: Wet weather wreaks havoc on Copper River roadways
summary: The confluence of rainy weather and warming climate has been busting up highways in Alaska's Copper River basin, triggering washouts, rock slides, broken culverts and floods. Most dramatic may be road closures this week along the Copper River on opposite sides of the Chugach Mountains. Taylor Highway was closed Wednesday, while high water threatened the Alaska and Richardson highways. An unstable slope at a rock slide south of Chitina prompted state officials to close the cliff-hugging road on Wednesday for the second time this summer, cutting off vehicle access to the popular salmon dipnetting area in Wood Canyon. "We are closing the road immediately and asking every one in there to come out," said George Levasseur, district maintenance manager for the state Department of Transportation. …

summary: | | | Pilot's remains recovered CRASH: Canadian team pulls body from 80-foot-deep crevasse. The Associated Press TY: Wire (Published: July 30, 2001) A Canadian search-and-rescue crew has recovered the body of an Alaska pilot killed when his plane crashed on a glacier in Kluane National Park, Canadian authorities said Sunday. Three Canadian Park Service helicopters were sent to the area, and two climbers helped pull Kurt Gloyer's body from a crevasse Saturday evening, according to Constable Kirk Gale. Two mountaineers who survived the crash were taken to an Anchorage hospital. One was released Saturday. The other remained hospitalized.
